title: CASS (Coding Agent Session Search) integration into AgenC
description:
## Summary

Integrate CASS (Coding Agent Session Search) into AgenC to give agents institutional memory — the ability to search past mission conversations, leverage cross-tool knowledge, and accumulate procedural memory over time.

CASS is a Rust tool by Jeffrey Emanuel (github.com/Dicklesworthstone/coding_agent_session_search) that indexes conversation history from 15+ AI coding agents into a single searchable knowledge base using Tantivy full-text search with BM25 scoring, edge n-gram tokenization, and optional semantic search via MiniLM.

## Motivation

Today, each AgenC mission starts from scratch — agents have no memory of past missions, no access to solutions discovered by other agents, and no accumulated knowledge base. CASS solves this by indexing all Claude Code session data (and other coding tools) into a searchable corpus that agents can query.

## Design

### Architecture Overview

CASS runs on the **host machine** (not inside ephemeral missions). AgenC agents access it via:

1. **MCP server** — CASS exposes MCP tools that agents can call directly
2. **CLI robot mode** — `cass search <query> --robot --limit N` for structured, token-budget-aware output

### Integration Layers

#### Layer 1: Session Search Across Missions

- Index Claude Code session data from `~/.claude/projects/` so agents can search past mission conversations
- CASS already supports Claude Code as a data source — AgenC needs to ensure the index stays fresh
- Agents query before starting work: "Has any past mission solved a similar problem?"

**Implementation:**
- Add CASS MCP server to the default agent MCP configuration in AgenC
- Create an AgenC skill (e.g., `/cass-search`) that wraps robot mode queries with sensible defaults
- Run `cass index` periodically (cron or on mission start) to keep the index current

#### Layer 2: Robot Mode as Agent Tool

- Agents invoke `cass search <query> --robot --limit N --min-score 0.5` to find relevant prior work
- Robot mode output is designed for agent consumption: structured, token-budget-aware
- Key flags: `--robot`, `--json`, `--limit`, `--min-score`, `--no-history`

**Implementation:**
- Register CASS binary path in AgenC config (`config.yml` key: `cass_binary_path`)
- Add MCP tool definition that wraps CASS CLI invocation
- Include in default agent permissions (sandbox allowlist for CASS binary + index directory)

#### Layer 3: CASS Memory System (Procedural Memory)

- The CASS Memory System (TypeScript/Bun) builds procedural memory on top of search
- Produces a `playbook.yaml` with confidence-tracked rules that mature through stages: hypothesis → emerging → established → core
- Rules represent accumulated knowledge: "When doing X in this codebase, always Y"

**Implementation:**
- Mount `playbook.yaml` as a read-only resource for agents (via CLAUDE.md injection or MCP resource)
- Run memory system extraction periodically to update the playbook
- AgenC config key for playbook path: `cass_playbook_path`
- Consider: should each repo have its own playbook, or one global playbook?

#### Layer 4: Cross-Repo Knowledge Transfer

- CASS indexes across all coding tools (Cursor, Codex, Aider, Gemini, etc.)
- Agents benefit from work done in other tools without any AgenC-specific setup
- This is "free" once CASS is indexing — the search corpus includes all tools

### Key Considerations

- **Dependency management:** CASS and the memory system (Bun) are external dependencies. AgenC should detect their presence and degrade gracefully if absent.
- **Index freshness:** Stale indexes reduce value. Options: index on mission start, periodic cron, or inotify-based incremental indexing.
- **Token budget:** Robot mode flags (`--limit`, `--min-score`) prevent overwhelming agent context windows. Default limits should be conservative.
- **Privacy/security:** CASS indexes raw conversation data. Users should be able to exclude specific directories or repos from indexing.
- **Performance:** Search should complete in <1s to avoid blocking agent startup.

### Phased Rollout

**Phase 1 (MVP):** Add CASS MCP server to default agent config. Agents can search past sessions manually.

**Phase 2:** Create `/cass-search` skill with sensible defaults. Auto-index on mission start if index is stale.

**Phase 3:** Integrate CASS Memory System playbook. Mount as agent knowledge. Periodic extraction.

**Phase 4:** Cross-tool knowledge graph. Relationship mapping between missions, repos, and discoveries.

## Success Criteria

- Agents can search past mission conversations and find relevant prior work
- Search results are token-budget-aware and don't overwhelm context windows
- CASS absence doesn't break AgenC (graceful degradation)
- Index stays reasonably fresh without manual intervention

## References

- CASS repo: github.com/Dicklesworthstone/coding_agent_session_search
- CASS Memory System: TypeScript/Bun layer that builds playbook.yaml
- Claude Code session data: ~/.claude/projects/

title: Allow claude remote-control for AgenC missions
description:
Enable Claude Code's remote-control feature (claude remote-control / /remote-control) so that AgenC mission agents can be monitored and controlled from mobile devices or browsers via claude.ai/code or the Claude mobile app.

Key details about the feature:
- Released in Claude Code v2.1.51 (Feb 24 2026), currently a research preview
- Start with 'claude remote-control' (new session) or '/remote-control' ('/rc') inside existing session
- Flags: --verbose, --sandbox / --no-sandbox
- Outbound HTTPS only (no inbound ports). Session registers with Anthropic API and polls for work. Traffic over TLS with short-lived credentials.
- Requires Max plan (Pro coming soon). API keys not supported. Must be signed in via /login.
- One remote session at a time per instance. Terminal must stay open. ~10 min network timeout.
- Can auto-enable for all sessions via /config -> Enable Remote Control for all sessions
- Many open bugs on GitHub (40+ issues)

What needs to happen for AgenC:
- Determine how to expose the remote-control capability to mission agents (CLI flag, config option, or auto-enable)
- Consider security implications of enabling remote access to agent sessions
- Handle the Max plan requirement gracefully (detect plan, warn if unsupported)
- Decide whether remote-control should be opt-in per mission or a global AgenC config setting
- Test interaction with AgenC tmux-based session management

Docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control
CLI ref: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference
Assessment: https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/25/claude-code-remote-control/

title: Fix non-rune-safe truncation in truncateWindowTitle
description: truncateWindowTitle in internal/wrapper/tmux.go uses collapsed[:maxLen-1] which slices by bytes not rune positions, corrupting multi-byte UTF-8 characters. The ellipsis appended is 3 bytes (U+2026) not 1, so result can exceed maxLen bytes. Fix: convert to []rune before slicing: string([]rune(collapsed)[:maxLen-1]) + '…'

title: Build 'mission manager' interactive TUI subcommand (Charm/Bubbletea)
description:
Add an interactive TUI subcommand (e.g. agenc mission manage or agenc mission tui) that presents a Bubbletea-powered interface for managing missions.

Core functionality:
- List all missions with status indicators (running, stopped, archived)
- Select missions via keyboard navigation
- Stop running missions
- If running from within AGENC_TMUX, also allow resuming missions (attach to tmux session)
- Show mission metadata (name, created date, agent template, etc.)

Technical approach:
- Use Charm Bubbletea (github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea) for the TUI framework
- Use Charm Lipgloss for styling
- Detect AGENC_TMUX environment variable to conditionally enable resume functionality
- Integrate with existing mission DB and tmux session management code

UX considerations:
- Keyboard shortcuts for common actions (s=stop, r=resume, q=quit, enter=details)
- Confirmation prompts for destructive actions (stop)
- Real-time status updates if feasible
- Filter/search capability for users with many missions

title: Dim palette command descriptions to dark gray

description: Have agenc continuously monitor running missions — analyzing session output, progress, and patterns — to detect when things aren't going well (e.g. agent is stuck, looping, making wrong decisions, hitting repeated errors). When issues are detected, proactively surface suggestions to the user on how to fix them (e.g. adjust the prompt, add a permission, provide context). Related to agenc-9pb (tmux driving) as it could use tmux access to read session output.

title: Create Claude plugin with skills to manipulate agenc from within a session
description: Build a Claude Code plugin that gives agents skills to interact with agenc from within their current session. Examples: spawn new missions using current session context, kick off investigation missions, query mission status, list running agents. This lets the user ask Claude to do agenc operations naturally in conversation rather than switching to a separate terminal.

title: Update README to mention hot-reloading once feature is re-implemented

title: Adopt a database schema migration tool
description: Replace the hand-rolled migration functions in internal/database/database.go (migrateWorktreeSourceToGitRepo, migrateAddLastHeartbeat, migrateAddShortID, etc.) with a proper schema migration tool. Evaluate options (golang-migrate, goose, atlas, etc.) and adopt one. Migrations should be versioned, ordered, and idempotent. This will make future schema changes safer and more maintainable as the number of migrations grows.

title: Extend background jobs with repo-orientation and GitHub Actions
description: Extend existing cron infrastructure so background crons always target a repo and create session branches (like interactive missions). Add GitHub Actions integration for interval-based background work (security audits, doc sync, test coverage, dependency updates). Background work is automatically captured in git. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Background Jobs' section.

title: Implement personal orchestrator agent
description: An always-running agent in its own tmux window that serves as the developer's control plane. Capabilities: query individual sessions ('what happened in auth-fix?'), query all sessions ('which are stuck?'), proactively surface lessons ('that looked like a bad pattern'), accept friction annotations via natural language. Aggregates multiple sessions into a single decision-making interface. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Personal Orchestrator Agent' in Tmux Control Plane section.

title: Implement pop/push mission stack
description: Tmux hotkeys (prefix + P for pop, prefix + U for push) for stack-based quick-switch between missions. Push saves current mission to a stack and switches to a target. Pop returns to the most recently pushed mission. Replaces ad-hoc tab-hunting. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Pop / Push' in Tmux Control Plane section.

title: Implement interrogate session hotkey
description: Tmux hotkey (prefix + I) that opens a side pane (tmux split) running a separate Claude instance with read-only access to the current session's JSONL history. Lets the developer ask questions about what happened without interrupting the running agent. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Interrogate' in Tmux Control Plane section.

title: Implement fork conversation hotkey
description:
Tmux hotkey (prefix + F) that snapshots the current session state tuple, creates a new tmux window with a forked mission starting from the same repo state (branch from current commit) and conversation history. Original session continues uninterrupted. Enables A/B testing prompts and exploring alternative approaches.

Subsumes agenc-304 (mission clone forking conversation history) and agenc-305/306 (copy session with new UUID, set conversation pointer).

Related: agenc-1g9 (turns table / state tuples provide the snapshot mechanism).

title: Implement friction capture system

Implement 'agenc friction <text>' command to annotate sessions with friction notes. Build friction analysis queries to find patterns across annotations. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Friction Capture System' section.

title: Build turn inspection commands
description: Build 'agenc turn ls' to list recorded turns for a mission (showing repo commit, config commit, timestamp) and 'agenc turn inspect <turn-id>' to display the full state tuple for a specific turn. Enables debugging and forensic analysis of agent interactions. See specs/hyperspace.md 'What This Enables' in Git State Capture section.

title: Create turns table and record reproducible state tuples
description: Create a 'turns' table in the database (id, mission_id, repo_commit, config_commit, cli_invocation, session_id, created_at). After each agent turn, record the full reproducible state tuple: repo commit from auto-commit, Claude config repo commit, CLI invocation string, and Claude session ID. Enables replay, debugging, A/B testing, and forensic analysis. See specs/hyperspace.md 'Git State Capture' section.

title: Build inter-agent message-passing system
description: Build an inter-agent message-passing system that allows AgenC missions to communicate with each other. Agents should be able to send messages to other agents (by mission UUID) and receive responses. When an agent initiates a conversation with another, it gets back the mission UUID which serves as the chat handle for ongoing communication.

title: Remove unused gitRepoName param from mission.CreateMissionDir

title: Research tmux -CC for iTerm integration
description: Research tmux -CC mode for iTerm integration. tmux -CC enables control mode where iTerm can manage tmux windows as native iTerm tabs/windows.

title: Passive polling of user GitHub repos for auto-discovery
description: Add passive polling of the user's GitHub repos so that users don't have to manually call 'agenc repo add'. AgenC would transparently pull down repos in the background, making them available without explicit registration.

title: Add lifecycle hooks for missions
description: Add hooks that fire during mission lifecycle events (create, start, stop, destroy) to allow users to create and tear down environments for missions. Examples: Edgy's devpods, test AgenC environments. This is about 'closing the agentic loop' — enabling human-out-of-the-loop automation of mission infrastructure.

title: Run missions in external directories
description: Allow spawning Claude sessions in directories that are NOT inside a workspaced mission. This enables using AgenC to manage Claude sessions in arbitrary directories on the user's machine.

title: Build custom permissions system via PreToolUse hook
description:
Build a PreToolUse hook that reimplements Claude Code's permissioning system, fixing its broken pattern matching. Claude Code's permission pattern matching is broken for multiline commands, heredocs, backticks, env var prefixes, and compound commands (&&, pipes, redirects). The * wildcard only matches within a single line, so patterns like Bash(gh issue create *) never match commands containing heredocs. Clicking 'Always allow' on these commands saves the entire verbatim heredoc into settings.json, corrupting it.

Core bugs (open, no Anthropic response):
- Multiline/heredoc matching: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11932
- Duplicate with deny-rule bypass: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25441
- Compound commands/redirects: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29421
- Env var prefixes break matching: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/8581
- Backticks in slash commands: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/8197

Settings corruption from 'Always allow':
- github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/15742
- github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16462
- github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25341

Feature requests:
- Parse compound commands: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16561
- Regex/glob support: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/13154
- Reduce heredoc usage: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11006
- Permission prompt suggests non-matching patterns: github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29967

Best workaround: PermissionRequest hook that receives full command JSON and implements custom matching. Reference implementations: gist.github.com/silenvx/5334afee5cd77e2dfcc1be4e943ad43b (Python) and blog.korny.info/2025/10/10/better-claude-code-permissions (Rust).

title: Share AgenC conversations read-only with others
description: Add the ability to share AgenC mission conversations as read-only with other people. This would have been useful for sharing research results (e.g. Yannik Claude research, Dé hamburger research). One possible implementation: export conversations as GitHub Gists.

title: Switch to git worktrees for mission isolation
description: Switch mission isolation from full repo clones to git worktrees. This is the equivalent of Gastown's refinery model — lighter weight, shared git objects, faster mission creation.

title: Remove separate agenc tmux requirement
description: Allow AgenC to work with any tmux session, rather than requiring a dedicated agenc tmux session. Users should be able to use AgenC within their existing tmux setup.

title: Build time-based usage limits and auto-disconnect
description: Allow users to set time-based boundaries on AgenC usage. This includes: disconnecting from the work tmux at a set time (e.g. 8pm), setting up movable disconnect times (e.g. afternoon walk), and generally limiting usage to healthy hours. Could be implemented via launchd jobs or internal scheduling.

title: Build mission cloning
description: Allow cloning an existing mission — its repo state, config, and context — into a new mission. Useful for analyzing friction points in past missions and for creating variants of existing work.

title: Visual prompt refinement UX with diff display
description:
[What]: Build a visual interface for prompt/skill refinement that shows diffs, reasoning for changes, and feedback loops — similar to what Charles McKay built independently with his "prompt factory" web app.
[Why]: AgenC's /refine-prompt command is CLI-only. Charles built a web-based prompt management tool with visual diffs, database-backed versioning, and a feedback loop where you describe what you wish had happened differently, see the proposed prompt diff with reasoning, and approve/reject. This is more accessible than CLI-based refinement for GUI-oriented users transitioning from tools like Cursor. His quote: "For all these AI tools, it's very black-boxed. And I want to know, like if I don't like what it outputted, how is it going to fix it? And what's the diff in its logic or instructions that would make it better the next time."
